_disclaimer: ALL ITEMS IN THIS FILE ARE SYNTHETIC PLACEHOLDERS. They are purpose-built stand-ins that demonstrate the import format. They are NOT real LSAT items and must NOT be used as study material. Real items must be sourced from official LSAC PrepTests (D-SR11). See SyntheticFlag field on every item.
_import_note: Fields map 1:1 to LSAT Item notetype fields (notetypes.md). 'SkillTag' may contain space-separated skill:: tags. 'SyntheticFlag' must be 'SYNTHETIC' for all items in this file.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] All registered voters who showed identification were allowed to vote. Since Maria was allowed to vote, she must have shown identification.
items.Stem: The reasoning above is flawed because it
items.ChoiceA: assumes that only registered voters would want to vote
items.ChoiceB: treats a sufficient condition as a necessary condition
items.ChoiceC: concludes that Maria is a registered voter without sufficient evidence
items.ChoiceD: fails to consider that some voters may have been turned away
items.ChoiceE: relies on an unverified premise about voter identification requirements
items.WhyWrongA: The argument says nothing about who wants to vote — this is out of scope.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Showing ID is sufficient to vote, but the argument treats it as necessary: because she voted, she must have shown ID. This reverses the conditional.
items.WhyWrongC: The argument doesn't conclude anything about registration status.
items.WhyWrongD: The argument is not about who was turned away; this is out of scope.
items.WhyWrongE: The premise is stipulated as given, not unverified in context.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Studies show that cities with more libraries have lower crime rates than cities with fewer libraries. Therefore, building more libraries will reduce crime.
items.Stem: The argument is most vulnerable to the criticism that it
items.ChoiceA: overstates the magnitude of the relationship between libraries and crime
items.ChoiceB: fails to consider that crime rates vary significantly by season
items.ChoiceC: mistakes a correlation for a causal relationship
items.ChoiceD: assumes that all libraries provide the same services
items.ChoiceE: ignores the possibility that crime rates have been declining nationally
items.WhyWrongA: The argument does not make claims about magnitude — this misidentifies the flaw.
items.WhyWrongB: Seasonal variation is unrelated to the argument's reasoning structure.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. The argument observes a correlation (more libraries, lower crime) and infers a causal direction (libraries cause less crime) without ruling out alternative explanations.
items.WhyWrongD: Library services are not relevant to this reasoning flaw.
items.WhyWrongE: National trends could explain the correlation, but this choice describes a missing premise, not the flaw — close but less precise than C.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] I interviewed five students at a local university who all reported studying fewer than four hours per week. Clearly, university students today are not putting in adequate study time.
items.Stem: The argument above is most vulnerable to which of the following criticisms?
items.ChoiceA: It assumes that adequate study time is the same for all students.
items.ChoiceB: It draws a conclusion about all university students from a small, potentially unrepresentative sample.
items.ChoiceC: It fails to specify what constitutes adequate study time.
items.ChoiceD: It does not consider that some students may work part-time jobs.
items.ChoiceE: It presupposes that studying is the primary determinant of academic success.
items.WhyWrongA: The argument's flaw is about sample size, not about the definition of 'adequate' — though A is a real consideration, it is not the primary logical error.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Five students at one university cannot support a claim about all university students — hasty generalization from an unrepresentative sample.
items.WhyWrongC: Lack of definition is a peripheral concern; the main flaw is the generalization.
items.WhyWrongD: Part-time work is out of scope for the argument's logical structure.
items.WhyWrongE: The argument makes no claims about determinants of academic success.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The new transit line will reduce commute times for workers traveling downtown. Therefore, workers will arrive at work less stressed and will be more productive.
items.Stem: The argument depends on the assumption that
items.ChoiceA: the transit line will be completed on schedule and within budget
items.ChoiceB: reduced commute time leads to less stress for commuters
items.ChoiceC: productivity is the most important measure of worker performance
items.ChoiceD: downtown workers currently experience significant commute delays
items.ChoiceE: the transit line will be used by a majority of downtown workers
items.WhyWrongA: Construction logistics are not assumed in moving from shorter commutes to less stress — out of scope for this step.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. The argument moves from 'shorter commute' to 'less stressed' — it must assume this connection holds. Negate it: 'shorter commutes do not reduce stress' → the conclusion collapses. Assumption Negation test passes.
items.WhyWrongC: The argument does not make productivity a primary metric; this is an irrelevant comparison.
items.WhyWrongD: Current delays are not assumed; the argument could hold even if delays are minor.
items.WhyWrongE: Usage rate is not required for the conclusion — even if only some workers use it, the logic still holds for them.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Sales of electric vehicles have increased every year for the past decade, and consumer awareness campaigns about climate change have also grown over the same period. The campaigns must be responsible for the rise in electric vehicle sales.
items.Stem: Which of the following is an assumption required by this argument?
items.ChoiceA: Electric vehicles are the most effective way to reduce carbon emissions.
items.ChoiceB: No other factors contributed significantly to the rise in electric vehicle sales.
items.ChoiceC: Consumer awareness campaigns are sponsored by electric vehicle manufacturers.
items.ChoiceD: The rise in electric vehicle sales is a desirable outcome for policymakers.
items.ChoiceE: Climate change awareness has increased more rapidly than electric vehicle sales.
items.WhyWrongA: Effectiveness of EVs for emissions reduction is out of scope; the argument is about the cause of their sales.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. To infer that the campaigns caused the sales increase, the argument must assume that no other explanation (falling battery costs, government subsidies, etc.) is responsible. Negation: 'Other factors did contribute significantly' → the correlation could be coincidental → conclusion fails.
items.WhyWrongC: Sponsorship is irrelevant to the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongD: Policy desirability is out of scope.
items.WhyWrongE: Relative growth rates are not assumed in the argument.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] All certified organic farms must submit annual soil reports. Greenfield Farm has not submitted a soil report this year.
items.Stem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?
items.ChoiceA: Greenfield Farm does not grow any organic produce.
items.ChoiceB: Greenfield Farm is not currently a certified organic farm.
items.ChoiceC: Greenfield Farm will lose its certification next year.
items.ChoiceD: Most certified organic farms submit their reports on time.
items.ChoiceE: The soil report requirement is new this year.
items.WhyWrongA: Growing organic produce does not require certification — a farm can grow organic produce without being certified. Out of scope.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Via contrapositive: If a farm does not submit a soil report → it is not a certified organic farm. Greenfield has not submitted → Greenfield is not certified. Valid deductive inference.
items.WhyWrongC: Future consequences (losing certification) go beyond what the stimulus supports — too extreme / not entailed.
items.WhyWrongD: 'Most farms' is a quantifier claim not supported by the stimulus, which says only 'all certified farms must submit.'
items.WhyWrongE: The newness of the requirement is not stated or inferable from the premises.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Some of the paintings in the museum were donated by private collectors. All paintings donated by private collectors have been authenticated. No painting that has been authenticated is available for loan.
items.Stem: Which of the following can be properly concluded from the statements above?
items.ChoiceA: All paintings in the museum have been authenticated.
items.ChoiceB: None of the paintings in the museum are available for loan.
items.ChoiceC: Some paintings in the museum are not available for loan.
items.ChoiceD: Some authenticated paintings were donated by private collectors.
items.ChoiceE: No private collector has donated a painting to any other museum.
items.WhyWrongA: Only some paintings were donated; we cannot conclude all are authenticated — too extreme.
items.WhyWrongB: We know only that some paintings are not available for loan (those donated by collectors); we cannot conclude none are — too extreme.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Some paintings → donated by collectors → authenticated → not available for loan. Via chained inference, some paintings are not available for loan. 'Some' claims survive the chain.
items.WhyWrongD: This reverses the direction: we know collector donations → authenticated, not that authenticated → collector donation. Reversal trap.
items.WhyWrongE: Other museums are entirely out of scope.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Every employee who completed the safety course received a certificate. No one who received a certificate was assigned to the night shift.
items.Stem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?
items.ChoiceA: All night-shift employees completed the safety course.
items.ChoiceB: No employee who completed the safety course was assigned to the night shift.
items.ChoiceC: Everyone assigned to the night shift received a certificate.
items.ChoiceD: Some employees who received certificates work the night shift.
items.ChoiceE: All employees received certificates.
items.WhyWrongA: This reverses the reasoning; nothing links night-shift workers to the course.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Completed course -> certificate -> not night shift. Chaining the conditionals, no course-completer is on the night shift.
items.WhyWrongC: This reverses the certificate conditional.
items.WhyWrongD: This contradicts the premise that certificate-holders are not on the night shift.
items.WhyWrongE: Only course-completers are said to receive certificates; not all employees.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The new tutoring program will improve student test scores, because students who attend tutoring spend more time reviewing material.
items.Stem: The argument depends on assuming which of the following?
items.ChoiceA: The tutoring program is free for all students.
items.ChoiceB: Spending more time reviewing material improves test scores.
items.ChoiceC: Tutoring is the only way to improve test scores.
items.ChoiceD: Students enjoy attending the tutoring program.
items.ChoiceE: Test scores are the best measure of learning.
items.WhyWrongA: Cost is out of scope for the step from 'more review' to 'better scores'.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Negate it — extra review does not improve scores — and the conclusion collapses, so it is required.
items.WhyWrongC: The argument need not claim tutoring is the ONLY route; that is too strong.
items.WhyWrongD: Enjoyment is irrelevant to whether scores improve.
items.WhyWrongE: Whether scores are the best measure is an irrelevant comparison.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Some critics claim the city's new recycling program is too expensive. But the program has already diverted thousands of tons of waste from landfills, and the long-term savings on landfill fees will more than offset its costs. The program is a wise investment.
items.Stem: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the main conclusion of the argument?
items.ChoiceA: The city's recycling program is too expensive.
items.ChoiceB: The program has diverted thousands of tons of waste from landfills.
items.ChoiceC: The city's recycling program is a wise investment.
items.ChoiceD: Long-term savings on landfill fees will offset the program's costs.
items.ChoiceE: Critics of the recycling program are wrong about everything.
items.WhyWrongA: This is the opposing view the author argues against, not the conclusion.
items.WhyWrongB: This is a premise offered in support of the conclusion.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. The final sentence states the author's overall judgment; the other claims support it.
items.WhyWrongD: This is a supporting premise, not the main point.
items.WhyWrongE: Overstated; the author disputes one claim, not everything critics say.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Regular exercise is often recommended for reducing stress. However, exercising too intensely can actually raise stress-hormone levels in the short term. Therefore, people seeking stress relief should favor moderate exercise over high-intensity workouts.
items.Stem: The main point of the argument is that
items.ChoiceA: exercise is commonly recommended for reducing stress.
items.ChoiceB: intense exercise can raise stress hormones in the short term.
items.ChoiceC: people seeking stress relief should favor moderate over high-intensity exercise.
items.ChoiceD: high-intensity workouts are dangerous to one's health.
items.ChoiceE: moderate exercise completely eliminates stress.
items.WhyWrongA: A premise setting up the argument, not its conclusion.
items.WhyWrongB: A premise; it supports the recommendation.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. The 'Therefore' sentence states the recommendation the argument builds toward.
items.WhyWrongD: Too strong; the argument only says intensity raises hormones short-term.
items.WhyWrongE: Too strong; the argument never claims stress is eliminated.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The museum plans to shorten its hours to save money. But most of the museum's revenue comes from evening events, which the shorter hours would eliminate. Cutting hours would therefore likely reduce the museum's overall income, not improve its finances.
items.Stem: Which one of the following most accurately states the conclusion of the argument?
items.ChoiceA: The museum plans to shorten its hours.
items.ChoiceB: Most of the museum's revenue comes from evening events.
items.ChoiceC: The museum should hold more evening events.
items.ChoiceD: Cutting the museum's hours would likely reduce its overall income.
items.ChoiceE: The museum is currently in serious financial trouble.
items.WhyWrongA: This is the plan being evaluated, not the author's conclusion.
items.WhyWrongB: A premise supporting the conclusion.
items.WhyWrongC: The author never recommends this; out of scope.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. The last sentence is the author's judgment that the plan backfires.
items.WhyWrongE: Not stated; the argument is about the effect of a specific plan.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Ravi: The city should build a new stadium; it will attract tourists and boost local businesses. Sofia: Public funds should not be spent on a stadium when that money could repair our crumbling schools.
items.Stem: Ravi and Sofia disagree over whether
items.ChoiceA: a new stadium would attract any tourists.
items.ChoiceB: the city should build a new stadium.
items.ChoiceC: the city's schools need repairs.
items.ChoiceD: stadiums ever benefit local businesses.
items.ChoiceE: public money should ever be spent on any construction.
items.WhyWrongA: Only Ravi speaks to tourism; Sofia takes no position on it.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Ravi favors building; Sofia opposes it — the point of disagreement.
items.WhyWrongC: Only Sofia mentions schools; Ravi does not dispute it.
items.WhyWrongD: 'Ever' overstates; neither makes a universal claim about all stadiums.
items.WhyWrongE: 'Ever...any' is too broad; Sofia objects to this spending, not all construction.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Dr. Lee: Antibiotics are overprescribed, which accelerates the rise of drug-resistant bacteria. Dr. Nguyen: Withholding antibiotics from patients who need them risks serious complications; doctors must be able to prescribe them freely.
items.Stem: The dialogue most strongly supports the claim that Lee and Nguyen disagree about whether
items.ChoiceA: drug-resistant bacteria can be dangerous.
items.ChoiceB: withholding antibiotics can cause complications.
items.ChoiceC: current antibiotic prescribing should be more restricted.
items.ChoiceD: antibiotics are ever medically necessary.
items.ChoiceE: bacteria are capable of developing resistance.
items.WhyWrongA: Neither disputes that resistant bacteria are dangerous.
items.WhyWrongB: Only Nguyen raises this; Lee does not deny it.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Lee wants less prescribing; Nguyen wants free prescribing — a direct clash.
items.WhyWrongD: 'Ever' is too strong; both accept antibiotics are sometimes needed.
items.WhyWrongE: This is common ground, not a disagreement.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Marcus: Remote work makes teams less productive, because the casual in-person interactions that spark the best ideas are lost. Elena: Studies show remote teams complete more tasks per week than in-office teams, so remote work is more productive.
items.Stem: Marcus and Elena disagree over whether
items.ChoiceA: casual interactions can ever spark good ideas.
items.ChoiceB: remote work reduces team productivity.
items.ChoiceC: remote teams complete any tasks at all.
items.ChoiceD: productivity can be measured in principle.
items.ChoiceE: in-office work should be banned.
items.WhyWrongA: Only Marcus relies on this; Elena does not deny idea-sparking interactions.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Marcus says remote work lowers productivity; Elena says it raises it.
items.WhyWrongC: Both accept remote teams complete tasks; not in dispute.
items.WhyWrongD: Neither questions whether productivity is measurable.
items.WhyWrongE: Out of scope; no one proposes banning office work.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Columnist: My opponent argues that lowering the speed limit will save lives. But when the neighboring state lowered its speed limit last year, its traffic fatalities did not decline at all. So the proposed reduction will not save lives here either.
items.Stem: The columnist's argument proceeds by
items.ChoiceA: attacking the opponent's character rather than the proposal.
items.ChoiceB: citing a comparable case in which the measure failed to produce the predicted effect.
items.ChoiceC: appealing to an authority on traffic safety.
items.ChoiceD: defining a key term more precisely than the opponent did.
items.ChoiceE: showing that the opponent's premises contradict each other.
items.WhyWrongA: No personal attack is made; the neighboring state is the evidence.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. The columnist uses an analogous case where the same measure did not work.
items.WhyWrongC: No authority is cited.
items.WhyWrongD: No term is redefined.
items.WhyWrongE: No internal contradiction is alleged.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Editor: Some say we should stop printing a paper edition to cut costs. Yet our surveys show that half our subscribers would cancel entirely without print. Eliminating print would thus cost us more in lost subscriptions than it would save.
items.Stem: The editor responds to the cost-cutting proposal by
items.ChoiceA: questioning the accuracy of the proposal's cost figures.
items.ChoiceB: arguing that the proposal would produce a result that defeats its own purpose.
items.ChoiceC: proposing an alternative way to cut costs.
items.ChoiceD: conceding the proposal while rejecting the motives behind it.
items.ChoiceE: attacking the competence of those who made the proposal.
items.WhyWrongA: The editor does not dispute the cost numbers.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. The editor shows the cost-cutting move would raise costs, undermining its goal.
items.WhyWrongC: No alternative measure is offered.
items.WhyWrongD: The editor rejects the proposal itself, not merely its motives.
items.WhyWrongE: No attack on people is made.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Theorist A: Human decisions are entirely determined by prior causes. Theorist B: If that were true, no one could be held responsible for their actions. But we clearly do hold people responsible, and rightly so. Therefore, A's claim must be false.
items.Stem: Theorist B responds to Theorist A by
items.ChoiceA: offering a single counterexample to A's general claim.
items.ChoiceB: showing that A's claim implies a consequence we reject, then rejecting the claim.
items.ChoiceC: presenting statistical evidence that contradicts A.
items.ChoiceD: redefining the notion of 'cause' to exclude decisions.
items.ChoiceE: appealing to the consensus of experts in the field.
items.WhyWrongA: B does not cite a specific counterexample; B derives an unacceptable implication.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. This is a reductio: A implies no responsibility, which we deny, so A is false.
items.WhyWrongC: No statistics are used.
items.WhyWrongD: B does not redefine 'cause'.
items.WhyWrongE: B appeals to a shared practice, not to experts.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Every member of the debate team is a senior. Priya is not a senior. So Priya is not a member of the debate team.
items.Stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
items.ChoiceA: All cats are mammals. Rex is a mammal. So Rex is a cat.
items.ChoiceB: Every published novel has an ISBN. This manuscript has no ISBN. So it is not a published novel.
items.ChoiceC: Most athletes train daily. Sam trains daily. So Sam is an athlete.
items.ChoiceD: All roses are flowers. This is a flower. So it may be a rose.
items.ChoiceE: No fish are mammals. A whale is a mammal. So a whale is not a fish.
items.WhyWrongA: This affirms the consequent (mammal, therefore cat) — an invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Same valid form: all X are Y; this is not Y; so this is not X (modus tollens).
items.WhyWrongC: Uses 'most' and affirms a property — invalid and different.
items.WhyWrongD: Draws only a 'may' conclusion from a converse — different structure.
items.WhyWrongE: Valid, but built on a negative universal premise — a different form.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] If the bakery raises prices, some regulars will leave. If some regulars leave, weekly revenue will fall. So if the bakery raises prices, weekly revenue will fall.
items.Stem: The reasoning in which one of the following is most parallel to the reasoning above?
items.ChoiceA: If it rains, the game is canceled. The game was canceled. So it rained.
items.ChoiceB: If the alarm rings, students exit. If students exit, the hall empties. So if the alarm rings, the hall empties.
items.ChoiceC: If you study, you pass. You did not study. So you did not pass.
items.ChoiceD: The store is open only if the lights are on. The lights are on. So the store is open.
items.ChoiceE: Either the door is locked or it is open. It is not locked. So it is open.
items.WhyWrongA: Affirms the consequent — invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Same valid chained conditionals (hypothetical syllogism).
items.WhyWrongC: Denies the antecedent — invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongD: Treats a necessary condition as sufficient — invalid.
items.WhyWrongE: Disjunctive syllogism — a different valid form.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] This restaurant must be excellent, because it is always crowded, and crowded restaurants are the good ones.
items.Stem: Which one of the following contains reasoning most similar to the flawed reasoning above?
items.ChoiceA: This book must be worth reading, since it is a bestseller, and bestsellers are the books worth reading.
items.ChoiceB: This medicine works, because a doctor prescribed it, and doctors understand medicine.
items.ChoiceC: The bridge is safe, because engineers inspected it thoroughly last week.
items.ChoiceD: She must be tired, since she yawned, and tired people yawn.
items.ChoiceE: The plan will succeed, because the team worked very hard on it.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Same 'popularity equals quality' equation used as a premise.
items.WhyWrongB: This is an appeal to authority — a different flaw.
items.WhyWrongC: This is not obviously flawed in the same way.
items.WhyWrongD: This affirms the consequent (yawned, so tired) — a different flaw.
items.WhyWrongE: This appeals to effort, not to popularity.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A company introduced standing desks and found that back-pain complaints dropped 30% over six months. It concluded that standing desks reduce back pain.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: Employees generally said they liked the new standing desks.
items.ChoiceB: During those six months, no other workplace change was made that could affect back pain.
items.ChoiceC: Standing for long periods can cause other health problems.
items.ChoiceD: Back pain is one of the most common complaints in office jobs.
items.ChoiceE: The company had also considered buying ergonomic chairs.
items.WhyWrongA: Liking the desks does not show they reduced pain.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Ruling out other causes makes the desks the likely reason for the drop.
items.WhyWrongC: Other harms do not support the pain-reduction claim; if anything they cut against it.
items.WhyWrongD: Background prevalence does not bear on the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongE: What was considered but not done is irrelevant.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] An ancient settlement was likely abandoned because of drought: sediment cores show a decades-long dry period beginning right around the time it was deserted.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most supports the hypothesis?
items.ChoiceA: Nearby settlements in wetter areas during the same era were not abandoned.
items.ChoiceB: The settlement contained many well-built stone houses.
items.ChoiceC: Sediment cores can be dated only to within a century.
items.ChoiceD: Droughts occurred repeatedly throughout the region's history.
items.ChoiceE: The settlement's population had been rising before the dry period.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. A contrast case tying abandonment specifically to drought supports the causal story.
items.WhyWrongB: Construction quality is irrelevant to why it was abandoned.
items.WhyWrongC: Loose dating weakens, not strengthens, the timing link.
items.WhyWrongD: If droughts were common without abandonment, the link is weakened.
items.WhyWrongE: Prior growth does not support drought as the cause of abandonment.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Films released in December win a disproportionate share of major awards. So releasing a film in December improves its chances of winning awards.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the reasoning?
items.ChoiceA: Among films of similar quality, December releases win more awards than releases in other months.
items.ChoiceB: December is one of the most popular months for going to the movies.
items.ChoiceC: Some award-winning films are released in the spring.
items.ChoiceD: Studios spend far more on marketing for their December releases.
items.ChoiceE: Most award voters now watch films on streaming platforms.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Holding quality fixed isolates timing as the factor, supporting the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongB: Ticket popularity does not bear on awards.
items.WhyWrongC: This slightly cuts against a strict December advantage.
items.WhyWrongD: Marketing spend is an alternative cause — it weakens the timing claim.
items.WhyWrongE: How voters watch is irrelevant to release timing.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Anyone who can lead this project must be both organized and persuasive. Dana is highly persuasive. Therefore, Dana can lead this project.
items.Stem: The conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?
items.ChoiceA: Dana is organized.
items.ChoiceB: Most persuasive people are also organized.
items.ChoiceC: Persuasiveness matters more than organization for leadership.
items.ChoiceD: Some organized people are persuasive.
items.ChoiceE: Dana has successfully led a project in the past.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. The requirement is organized AND persuasive; adding that Dana is organized makes the conclusion follow.
items.WhyWrongB: 'Most' does not guarantee Dana specifically is organized.
items.WhyWrongC: A ranking of traits does not supply the missing 'organized' condition.
items.WhyWrongD: 'Some' does not establish that Dana is organized.
items.WhyWrongE: Past success is out of scope and does not establish organization.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The proposal will pass only if it has the mayor's support. The mayor supports any proposal that reduces the city deficit. This proposal reduces the deficit. Therefore, the proposal will pass.
items.Stem: The conclusion is properly drawn if which one of the following is assumed?
items.ChoiceA: Any proposal that the mayor supports will pass.
items.ChoiceB: The mayor supports only proposals that reduce the deficit.
items.ChoiceC: The proposal faces no organized opposition on the council.
items.ChoiceD: Reducing the deficit is the mayor's highest priority.
items.ChoiceE: The proposal will pass only if the mayor supports it.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. We can derive the mayor's support; adding 'supported -> passes' completes the chain to 'will pass.'
items.WhyWrongB: This reverses the deficit conditional and does not yield passage.
items.WhyWrongC: Absence of opposition is out of scope and not sufficient.
items.WhyWrongD: Priorities do not guarantee the proposal passes.
items.WhyWrongE: This merely restates the given necessary condition; it does not make passage follow.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The company will be profitable this year if it lands the Henderson contract. Landing that contract requires a strong proposal. The company has submitted a strong proposal. Therefore, the company will be profitable this year.
items.Stem: The conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?
items.ChoiceA: If the company submits a strong proposal, it will land the Henderson contract.
items.ChoiceB: The Henderson contract is the company's largest contract.
items.ChoiceC: The company has never lost a contract it bid on.
items.ChoiceD: A strong proposal is necessary to land the contract.
items.ChoiceE: The company will be profitable only if it lands the contract.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. A strong proposal is only stated to be necessary; assuming it is also sufficient yields the contract, hence profitability.
items.WhyWrongB: Contract size is out of scope.
items.WhyWrongC: A perfect record is too strong and does not connect this proposal to this contract.
items.WhyWrongD: This restates the given necessary condition; necessity is not sufficiency.
items.WhyWrongE: This reverses the profitability conditional.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] After the town installed brighter streetlights, nighttime traffic accidents fell. Clearly, the brighter lights made the roads safer.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: The town also lowered the nighttime speed limit at the same time.
items.ChoiceB: The brighter streetlights cost more than the town expected.
items.ChoiceC: Residents said they felt safer after the lights were installed.
items.ChoiceD: Daytime accident rates were unchanged over the period.
items.ChoiceE: A few drivers complained that the new lights were too bright.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. A simultaneous speed-limit cut is an alternative cause of the accident drop.
items.WhyWrongB: Cost has no bearing on whether the lights caused fewer accidents.
items.WhyWrongC: Feeling safer does not show the lights caused the decline.
items.WhyWrongD: Unchanged daytime rates are neutral to the nighttime claim.
items.WhyWrongE: A few complaints do not address the causal claim.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A vitamin maker claims its supplement boosts immunity, noting that people who take it report fewer colds than people who do not.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most undermines the claim?
items.ChoiceA: People who take the supplement also tend to exercise more and eat better.
items.ChoiceB: The supplement is sold in most major pharmacies.
items.ChoiceC: Colds are generally more frequent during winter months.
items.ChoiceD: The supplement contains both vitamin C and zinc.
items.ChoiceE: Some people who take the supplement still catch colds.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Healthier habits among users is an alternative explanation for the fewer colds.
items.WhyWrongB: Availability is irrelevant to effectiveness.
items.WhyWrongC: Seasonality affects both groups and does not explain the difference.
items.WhyWrongD: Ingredients do not by themselves undermine the comparison.
items.WhyWrongE: 'Fewer colds' is consistent with some users still getting colds.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A historian argues that the empire fell because of economic decline, pointing to shrinking trade records in its final century.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the historian's argument?
items.ChoiceA: Many trade records from that century were later destroyed, so surviving records understate actual trade.
items.ChoiceB: The empire is known today for its impressive architecture.
items.ChoiceC: Economic decline can result from a wide variety of causes.
items.ChoiceD: The historian has studied the empire for several decades.
items.ChoiceE: Trade was an important part of the empire's economy.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. If records were lost, shrinking records may not reflect shrinking trade — the key evidence is undercut.
items.WhyWrongB: Architecture is irrelevant to the economic-decline claim.
items.WhyWrongC: General causal variety does not address this argument's evidence.
items.WhyWrongD: The historian's experience is an appeal, not evidence about trade.
items.WhyWrongE: That trade mattered is background that, if anything, supports the argument.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Our new hiring test predicts job success: 80% of employees who scored high on it later received strong performance reviews.
items.Stem: Which of the following would be most useful to know in evaluating the argument?
items.ChoiceA: What percentage of employees who scored low on the test also received strong reviews.
items.ChoiceB: How long the hiring test takes an applicant to complete.
items.ChoiceC: Whether employees found the hiring test enjoyable.
items.ChoiceD: How much it costs the company to administer the test.
items.ChoiceE: Which consultant originally designed the hiring test.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Without the low-scorers' base rate, the 80% figure cannot show the test predicts anything.
items.WhyWrongB: Test length does not bear on predictive validity.
items.WhyWrongC: Enjoyment is irrelevant to prediction.
items.WhyWrongD: Cost is irrelevant to whether the test predicts success.
items.WhyWrongE: The designer's identity is irrelevant.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The city claims its anti-litter campaign worked: litter complaints dropped 40% after the campaign launched.
items.Stem: The answer to which of the following questions would be most relevant to evaluating the claim?
items.ChoiceA: Did the city reduce the number of ways residents could report litter during that period?
items.ChoiceB: How much money did the anti-litter campaign cost to run?
items.ChoiceC: Did any other cities run similar anti-litter campaigns?
items.ChoiceD: What slogan did the campaign use in its advertisements?
items.ChoiceE: How many residents said they remembered seeing the campaign?
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. If reporting channels shrank, fewer complaints may not mean less litter.
items.WhyWrongB: Cost is irrelevant to whether litter actually fell.
items.WhyWrongC: Other cities' campaigns do not bear on this city's result.
items.WhyWrongD: The slogan is irrelevant to the drop's meaning.
items.WhyWrongE: Recall is weakly related but does not test whether complaints track litter.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A manager argues that new software sped up order processing, since average processing time dropped after it was installed.
items.Stem: Which of the following would be most useful to determine in order to evaluate the manager's argument?
items.ChoiceA: Whether the volume or complexity of orders changed when the software was installed.
items.ChoiceB: How much the software's annual license costs.
items.ChoiceC: Whether employees found the new software easy to use.
items.ChoiceD: Which vendor supplied the new software.
items.ChoiceE: Whether the software also has a mobile version.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. If orders got simpler or fewer, that — not the software — could explain the faster times.
items.WhyWrongB: License cost is irrelevant to processing speed.
items.WhyWrongC: Ease of use does not establish the cause of the time drop.
items.WhyWrongD: The vendor's identity is irrelevant.
items.WhyWrongE: A mobile version is irrelevant to the claim.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A coffee shop raised its prices last month, yet its total revenue from coffee increased even though it sold fewer cups than before.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy?
items.ChoiceA: The higher price per cup more than offset the smaller number of cups sold.
items.ChoiceB: The shop also began selling pastries for the first time last month.
items.ChoiceC: Several customers complained about the price increase.
items.ChoiceD: A competing café nearby closed down last month.
items.ChoiceE: The shop reduced its opening hours last month.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. A large enough price rise can raise coffee revenue despite fewer cups sold.
items.WhyWrongB: Pastry sales explain total store revenue, not coffee revenue specifically.
items.WhyWrongC: Complaints do not explain how revenue rose.
items.WhyWrongD: A closed competitor would raise cups sold, contradicting 'fewer cups.'
items.WhyWrongE: Fewer hours would tend to lower sales, deepening the puzzle.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Despite eating a diet high in cheese and butter, the people of a certain region have unusually low rates of heart disease.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, does most to resolve the apparent paradox?
items.ChoiceA: Residents of the region walk many miles each day and eat large amounts of fresh vegetables.
items.ChoiceB: Cheese and butter are among the region's most popular foods.
items.ChoiceC: Heart disease is the leading cause of death worldwide.
items.ChoiceD: The region imports most of its dairy products from abroad.
items.ChoiceE: The region's cuisine is admired by chefs internationally.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Heavy activity and vegetables supply a protective factor offsetting the rich diet.
items.WhyWrongB: This restates the puzzling fact without resolving it.
items.WhyWrongC: Worldwide statistics do not explain this region's low rate.
items.WhyWrongD: Where dairy comes from does not affect its health impact.
items.WhyWrongE: Culinary reputation is irrelevant to heart-disease rates.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The library installed self-checkout machines to reduce staff workload, but staff now report being busier than before.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the surprising result?
items.ChoiceA: Since the machines were installed, library visits have risen sharply, increasing all staff duties.
items.ChoiceB: The self-checkout machines were cheaper than hiring additional staff.
items.ChoiceC: Some patrons still prefer to check out books with a staff member.
items.ChoiceD: The machines occasionally jam and need a quick reset.
items.ChoiceE: Staff attended a one-time training session on the machines.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. A surge in visitors raises other duties, so staff are busier despite the machines.
items.WhyWrongB: Cost savings do not explain increased busyness.
items.WhyWrongC: A minority preferring staff checkout is too small to explain the effect.
items.WhyWrongD: Occasional resets are minor and would not outweigh the labor saved.
items.WhyWrongE: One-time training does not explain ongoing greater busyness.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Nina borrowed her neighbor's ladder without asking, but returned it in perfect condition before the neighbor even noticed. Nina did nothing wrong.
items.Stem: Which one of the following principles, if valid, most helps to justify the reasoning above?
items.ChoiceA: An action is wrong only if it causes harm to someone.
items.ChoiceB: Borrowing anything without asking is always wrong.
items.ChoiceC: People should always ask permission before borrowing.
items.ChoiceD: Returning borrowed items promptly is a virtue.
items.ChoiceE: One should treat others' property as if it were one's own.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. If wrongness requires harm and no harm occurred, then Nina did nothing wrong.
items.WhyWrongB: This contradicts the conclusion that Nina did nothing wrong.
items.WhyWrongC: This cuts against the conclusion rather than supporting it.
items.WhyWrongD: Prompt return being virtuous does not establish that the borrowing was permissible.
items.WhyWrongE: A general care principle does not justify borrowing without asking.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Principle: A public official should recuse herself from any decision in which she has a personal financial stake.
items.Stem: Which one of the following judgments conforms most closely to the principle stated above?
items.ChoiceA: Councilor Ruiz, who owns shares in BuildCo, abstains from the vote on BuildCo's city contract.
items.ChoiceB: Councilor Ruiz votes on BuildCo's contract because she believes BuildCo is the best choice.
items.ChoiceC: Councilor Ruiz recuses herself from a vote on a park design she personally dislikes.
items.ChoiceD: Councilor Ruiz discloses her BuildCo shares but votes on the contract anyway.
items.ChoiceE: Councilor Ruiz sells her BuildCo shares the day after the vote.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. She has a financial stake and recuses — exactly what the principle requires.
items.WhyWrongB: Voting despite a financial stake violates the principle.
items.WhyWrongC: Personal dislike is not a financial stake.
items.WhyWrongD: Disclosure is not recusal; she still votes with a stake.
items.WhyWrongE: Selling afterward does not satisfy recusal at the time of the decision.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A teacher lets a student retake an exam the student missed because of a documented illness, but refuses a retake to a student who simply overslept.
items.Stem: Which one of the following principles is best illustrated by the teacher's decisions?
items.ChoiceA: Students should be accommodated for missed work only when the cause was beyond their control.
items.ChoiceB: Students should always be given a second chance on an exam.
items.ChoiceC: Teachers should never permit exam retakes under any circumstances.
items.ChoiceD: Documented excuses are more persuasive than undocumented ones.
items.ChoiceE: Students who oversleep deserve harsher penalties than other students.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Illness (beyond control) earns a retake; oversleeping (avoidable) does not — the principle both decisions share.
items.WhyWrongB: This contradicts the refusal in the second case.
items.WhyWrongC: This contradicts the granted retake in the first case.
items.WhyWrongD: The distinction is control, not documentation as such.
items.WhyWrongE: The teacher withholds a retake but imposes no extra penalty.
